text: Consider a patient positioned for left upper lobectomy using the anterior two incision technique cameras in the eighth inner space and a single utility incision.
Port placement is shown here.
Scapular tip is a helpful landmark.
The camera in the eighth inner space and a single four centimeter utility incision.
Upon entering the chest we begin with the posterior dissection.
The lung is reflected anteriorly and using electrocautery and careful dissection we take down the posterior pleural reflection.
This is exposing the pulmonary artery and careful retrieval of lymph nodes along the way allows for us to identify branches.
While it is sometimes possible to divide branches posteriorly, generally we will perform all of the dissection and division of major structures from the anterior approach.
Next, we must carefully identify the superior and inferior pulmonary veins, similarly opening the pleural reflection on the anterior aspect of the lung.
Blunt dissection and electrocautery is used to facilitate this dissection.
Once the superior most aspect of the superior vein has been exposed, dissection here begins the separation between the pulmonary artery.
Remember, in a left upper lobectomy, the pulmonary artery takes a sweeping curve, and the veins and bronchus are nestled within this.

It's helpful with the harking clamp to begin the dissection pointed towards the pericardium, and then pulling this gently towards the utility incision and sweeping superiorly allows for careful separation.
A better stapler angle is frequently available from the camera port so the camera is moved to the utility incision for this part of the dissection.
And as shown here, if the stapler doesn't pass easily, take it back and make sure that it goes easily between the vein and the bronchus.
It's important to fixate and control the stapler at the insertion point on the skin to minimize unnecessary movement during firing of the stapler.
After a division of the vein, we now direct our attention to the bronchus, which is the next structure, and we are looking for the lymph nodes.
This is a level 11 lymph node, which marks the secondary carina.
This is sometimes higher than expected, and it's important to carefully identify this to avoid inadvertently stapling the main stem bronchus.
Once the bronchus is carefully separated from the pulmonary artery, it can now be encircled.
Usually it is not necessary, but keep in mind that sometimes a vessel loop or silk tie can be passed to help elevate the bronchus out of the curvature of the pulmonary artery.
But again, as is shown here, ongoing dissection along the bronchus to free up as much as possible of the tissue which is keeping these together will allow for safe stapling of the bronchus.
This view here illustrates the point that the bronchus must be elevated from the pulmonary artery during manipulation, and in particular during firing of the stapler.
The camera will again be relocated to the utility incision as shown here, and prior to passing the stapler, I will generally pass an instrument to confirm that the bronchus is indeed free.

Now, with the stapler appropriately articulated, it is passed behind the bronchus, and with counter-rotation of the body of the stapler, the bronchus is elevated from the curvature of the pulmonary artery, which allows this to be stapled.
Note again at this point, after the staple firing is complete, the stapler is stabilized and rotated gently away from the hilum and removed under direct vision.

A locking grasper is now placed on the divided bronchus, which is elevated from the ongoing pulmonary artery, and this allows for careful retrieval of lymph nodes and alveolar tissue to expose the pulmonary artery and its branches.
Remember, in a left upper lobectomy, anatomy is not necessarily conserved, and there may be anything from two to up to seven individual branches that need to be accounted for.
So careful dissection in this area and judicious use of the two available stapling ports allows these to be taken comfortably and safely.
In this case, the stapler is once again introduced from the camera incision.
And at this point, it's also good to keep in mind that upper traction must be minimized to avoid avulsing a branch at this point during firing of the stapler.
This patient had a large posterior branch and a second smaller branch, which we had seen earlier.

Again, keep in mind, having completed the posterior dissection at the beginning of the case means that we do not need to constantly go back to confirm that this area is free as we have performed this at the beginning of the case.
Finally, as this technique is a fissureless or a fissure-last technique, we introduce a stapler and take the fissure.
Leaving the locking grasper on the bronchus and a secondary grasper to move the lung parenchyma, the stapler is introduced with the anvil pointed superiorly and away from the hilum.
Once it is closed, the camera pilot introduces the camera to visualize the ongoing pulmonary artery prior to completing firing of the stapler across the fissure.
This usually takes a couple of staple fires, and again, it's important to be on the lookout for any pulmonary arterial branches which may not have been divided during the hyaluronid dissection.
A retrieval bag is introduced towards the apex.
The specimen is then placed within this.
And during removal of the lung, remember that the lung is deformable, so sometimes using the bag as a wound protector and gently extruding the lung through this will minimize trauma.
Finally, the lymphadenectomy is completed as detailed elsewhere in the course.
Level 7 lymph nodes can be and should be retrieved from the left side of the chest.
I generally perform a final air leak test under sterile water.
The camera can be introduced and visualize the bronchial stump.
And a single 24 French chest tube is exteriorized through the camera incision.
We hope you find this video helpful and look forward to discussing this more in person at the course.
